title: Communicate With Confidence: The Blueprint For Mastering Every Conversation
description: In this video, tech CEO Sandeep Swadia breaks down the 8 mental and communication shifts that every business leader, manager, and ambitious professional needs to master.
transcript:
Every breakthrough or breakdown in our
life starts with a single conversation.
We're all trying to become better
communicators. Even though I've been a
CEO and I grew up on stage as a
musician, I've often fumbled and failed
when the stakes were high. These eight
powerful mental shifts transformed the
way I communicated and they may help you
as well. Let's get started. First up,
communicating is the hardest thing to do
for humans. We don't actually hear what
people say. We hear what our brain wants
to hear. Research from neuroscientist
Lisa Felman Barrett shows that the human
brain is a prediction machine. It's not
a tape recorder. It fills in the meaning
before the sentence is even complete.
This pattern matching and prediction is
something that evolution perhaps wired
into us to decrease our response time or
increase our chance of survival. It
makes sense, right? If the tiger is
jumping at me, I better be pattern
matching very quickly. So this is why
when the great Jimmyi Hendris famously
sang, "While I kiss the sky, excuse me,
while I kiss the sky," millions of us
heard, "While I kiss this guy, true
story." And here's the thing, there are
more than 7,000 living languages in the
world today. So if my brain can only
decode in Spanish and yours can only
communicate in Swahili, we would need a
translator before we can communicate
with each other, right? But the same
translation gap exists when we speak in
the same language because there are four
layers that distort every message. What
you meant to what you said to what they
heard to what they interpreted. What you
meant isn't what they heard and what
they heard isn't what they will
remember. Next is number two. The medium
complicates the message. As a general
rule, call when it matters, text when
it's safe. Of course, we all love
texting. It's super convenient. How many
times have you crafted that perfect text
and perfect email only to see it blow up
in your face? Why? Because it wasn't
received as well as you had intended.
So, this is the second shift you have to
make. Tone doesn't travel through text.
Meaning gets scrambled through the
medium. So, you must match the medium to
the moment, to the message. Here's a
simple framework I used to help me
choose the right medium. CPR,
complexity, pressure, response. When the
topic is complex or the emotional stakes
are high or it requires a two-way
conversation, it's better to talk. If at
least two of those three are high, it's
better to pick up the phone or meet in
person. If you're giving feedback, if
you're resolving conflict or giving
comfort to someone, it's better not to
do it over text. A lot of the misfires
and drama in my life would have been
completely avoided if only I had a real
conversation instead of texted. You're
typing on a device that is designed for
your voice and emotions. On to number
three. Are you really listening? Chris
Rock has this great line. People don't
really listen. They're just waiting for
their turn to talk. This happens to me
all the time. I nod politely and I act
like I'm a very good listener. Listening
is not about nodding politely or acting
like you're a great listener. It's a
full body mind connection of being there
for that person, not playing your own
record over their song. If you want them
to understand you, they first have to
feel understood. But I use this
listening mixer framework as if I am a
DJ and I have a mixer and I have these
four buttons. The first button is a mute
button. If someone starts speaking to
you, try not to think about what you'll
say next. Mute. Second is the pause
button. After they're done talking, take
a breath. Let there be silence. Give
your brain some time to process what you
just heard. Third, record button. Read
their tone, their energy, their emotion
to understand the context. Mirror what
they have said to build some trust. And
the fourth is the playback button. Only
now can we speak? Because now when we do
it, we'll speak in the same emotional
key and that will build comfort that
will build trust. Number four is next.
Are you hiding behind your words without
even realizing it? Long time ago, my
boss gave me perhaps one of the most
critical feedback of my entire career.
He said, "Sandep, you hide behind your
humor. you deflect. You use sarcasm
instead of cander and it's going to make
people unsure of who you are and how to
trust you. And that hit me like a truck.
And he was right. I was hiding behind
jokes because I was insecure. And some
of us hide behind jargon. And there is
enough research done on this concept of
cognitive load theory which says that
the harder someone has to work to
understand you, the less they will
remember what you said. So try to be
simple. And here are some things you can
do immediately. Instead of saying
leverage, say use. Instead of saying
strategy, say plan, simplify. Next is
number five. And this affects everyone.
Panic under pressure. Most people panic
when asked a tough question. When I was
leading large teams, I would sit through
endless meetings where I would catch
someone or even myself just rambling on
and on instead of directly answering the
question. So we created a framework
called the art of the answer to teach
folks to speak clearly when they're
under pressure. So apply this framework
in meetings or interviews or any
highstakes conversation. Art is a art. A
stands for answer the question. But
don't build up to the punch line. Start
with it. Just answer the question
directly and precisely. R stands for the
reveal. Now add a reason or a story or
an insight. Now explain the why behind
the what? Something that adds depth and
context. Tie it back. You took off. Now
land the plane. Loop back to the
original question and show clarity. When
you are in any high-tech situation, in a
job interview, in a tough client call,
or in a boardroom, use this art of the
answer to be direct, precise, and
concise. And in those moments, clarity
becomes a gift to you, to them, to the
room. The sixth tip is all about what to
do when you freeze. I remember when I
was young, I was working as an equity
analyst at an investment bank. I had my
first highstakes client call and they
were a huge hedge fund worth billions of
dollars and the fund manager asked me a
question about a financial model that we
had sent over. And yes, this was my turn
to be under pressure and answering
someone who had asked a very tough
question. Now, I was supposed to be the
expert. And I didn't know the answer. I
just completely froze. And in the middle
of the conversation, click. I just hung
up. Yeah, that happened. That's what
panic does to you. It's just a complete
freeze. But I knew what to do from my
monk days. So, I did some deep
breathing. I calmed down. I walked over
to my boss. I told him what had
happened. He walked me through how to
answer the question. I walked back. I
picked up the phone. I called the client
and I gave him the answer. And he was so
surprised that I had hung up in the
middle of a conversation. But he was
also happy that we were taking care of
him by sharing our best thinking. So
panic will hijack your brain. Your
preffrontal cortex goes offline during
panic. And when that happens, find a way
to calm down, center yourself, own your
mistakes, and find a solution. High
performers don't shine because they
never panic. They shine because they
don't fall apart when that happens.
Presence beats perfection. Number seven,
your voice is your signature. Here's
what we never learn in school. The
quality of your voice starts with the
quality of your breath. I learned three
things from this great opera singer in
Boston. Number one, speak from your
core. And if you can't, then speak from
your chest because that will improve the
resonance of your voice. Number two,
find your right vocal register that
makes your voice sound attractive. Do
you want to talk like this or do you
want to talk like that? It depends on
your voice and your choice. And you can
vary the pitch from all the way here,
all the way there. But you got to pick
the right register. And number three,
control the ending. So a lot of folks
that I know have this question mark
style of talking where every sentence
ends at a high note. Human ears equate
that to questioning. So here's a simple
practice. Record yourself. Audio, video,
listen to it, review it, apply those
three ideas. Because in business, in
leadership, in life, your voice is your
instrument. And the more you learn to
play that instrument with grace, with
power, with fluidity, the more impact
your communication will have. So, you
got to harness its power. Last is number
eight. The world doesn't need more
noise. It needs better conversation. How
many of us remember the full speech that
Dr. Martin Luther King gave that day? I
don't. All we remember are those four
magical words. I have a dream. I have a
dream. Four words that are etched in
America's psyche. The words mattered
because we could feel the presence and
the emotion behind them. When I was a
CEO, I had this simple practice. When
someone walked into my office, I would
close the laptop or physically turn away
the computer screen and just sat in
front of them. It told the person who
walked in that now you have my full
attention. That's the deepest truth
about communication because attention is
the rarest form of love that if you want
to master communication then give your
attention, give your emotions, give your
intention to the other person.
Generosity makes you the best
communicator. Just try to focus on the
next conversation fully and without
judgment because who knows that one
conversation could change someone's
life. So these were the eight shifts
that helped me change the way I
communicated. I hope you found them
useful. What strategies and tactics have
you seen or implemented? Please share.
If you found this video helpful, please
subscribe. That way you'll communicate
to YouTube algorithm that these ideas
can help others as well. Also check out
my video on how to build a life that's
rich in every way. Building a career,
building a business, and of course
building wealth. Thank you and I love
